content:
# Genesis Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6r–6v | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_010_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_010_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 7–8 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** These are the generations of Noah's sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Sons were born to them after the flood.

---

## The Line of Japheth

**2** The sons of Japheth:

Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.

**3** The sons of Gomer:

Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah.

**4** The sons of Javan:

Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim.

**5** From these the coastland peoples spread out into their territories — each with their own language, by their clans, within their nations.

---

## The Line of Ham

**6** The sons of Ham:

Cush, Egypt, Put, and Canaan.

**7** The sons of Cush:

Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, and Sabteca.

The sons of Raamah:

Sheba and Dedan.

**8** Cush also fathered Nimrod — and Nimrod was the first man on earth to become a mighty warrior. **9** He was a mighty hunter before the LORD — which is why people still say, *"Like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD."* **10** The beginning of his kingdom was Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh — all of them in the land of Shinar. **11** From that land he went out into Assyria and built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah, **12** and Resen — between Nineveh and Calah: that is the great city.

**13** Egypt fathered the Ludites, the Anamites, the Lehabites, the Naphtuhites, **14** the Pathrusites, the Casluhites — from whom the Philistines came — and the Caphtorites.

**15** Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn, and Heth, **16** and the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Girgashites, **17** the Hivites, the Arkites, the Sinites, **18** the Arvadites, the Zemarites, and the Hamathites. Afterward the Canaanite clans scattered abroad, **19** so that the Canaanite territory ran from Sidon, going toward Gerar all the way to Gaza, and then toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, as far as Lasha.

**20** These are the sons of Ham, by their clans, their languages, their lands, their nations.

---

## The Line of Shem

**21** And to Shem also — the elder brother of Japheth, the ancestor of all the sons of Eber — sons were born.

**22** The sons of Shem:

Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, and Aram.

**23** The sons of Aram:

Uz, Hul, Gether, and Mash.

**24** Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber.

**25** Two sons were born to Eber: the name of the first was Peleg — because in his days the earth was divided — and his brother's name was Joktan.

**26** Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah, **27** Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah, **28** Obal, Abimael, Sheba, **29** Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab. All these were the sons of Joktan.

**30** Their territory stretched from Mesha in the direction of Sephar, the hill country of the east.

**31** These are the sons of Shem, by their clans, their languages, their lands, their nations.

---

## The Conclusion of the Table

**32** These are the clans of Noah's sons, according to their genealogies, within their nations — and from these the nations spread out across the earth after the flood.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On Genesis 10 as a whole — the world arranged by name

Genesis 10 is the most disorienting chapter in the early pages of the Bible — and the most deliberately structured. Seventy names. Seventy nations. A world sorted not by conquest or chronology but by kinship: every people has a father, every territory has a family, every language has an ancestor. This is the biblical imagination's most radical act of unity-claiming: all human diversity traces back to one surviving family, and before that to one God.

This is not genealogy in the modern sense — a genetic record. It is political and theological cartography. "Nations" (*goyim*), "languages" (*leshonot*), "lands" (*aratsot*), "clans" (*mishpehot*) — these four categories appear as a refrain at the end of each major section (vv. 5, 20, 31, 32), deliberately organizing the human family as a map of belonging, not a hierarchy of worth. The text holds all seventy in a single frame without valuing any above another — an astonishing theological restraint.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6r–6v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 7–8 (pa_0007, pa_0008).*

---

### Verse 1 — the fourth toledot

**אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת בְּנֵי נֹחַ** (*'elleh toledot bene-Noach*) — "These are the generations of Noah's sons." The *toledot* formula — "these are the generations of" — has appeared four times already in Genesis: Adam (5:1), heavens and earth (2:4), Noah (6:9). Each use marks a new phase in the story. Here it signals that we have moved from Noah's story to the world his sons generated. The brief addendum "sons were born to them after the flood" is doing more than it seems: it is anchoring everything that follows — every name, every nation, every language — to what happened *after* the cataclysm. The world is not the remnant of something destroyed. It is something newly born.

---

### Verses 2–5 — Japheth: the coastlands and the far horizon

Japheth's sons stretch from the Black Sea region (Gomer = Cimmerians, Ashkenaz = Scythians, Togarmah = Armenia/Anatolia) to the Mediterranean (Javan = Ionia/Greece, Kittim = Cyprus/coastlands, Tarshish = perhaps Spain or Sardinia). Madai = the Medes. Magog and Meshech and Tubal appear later in Ezekiel's apocalyptic visions as forces at the rim of the known world. The "coastland peoples" (*'iyye ha-goyim*, literally "islands of the nations") became a biblical idiom for distant, sea-faring peoples — the world beyond the horizon.

The refrain "each with their own language, by their clans, within their nations" (v. 5) will be pointedly subverted in chapter 11, where Babel represents the anti-Table: one language, one location, one people refusing to scatter. Chapter 10 is the world as God intends it — diverse, spread, named. Chapter 11 is the human attempt to undo that. Read in order, the Table of Nations makes Babel's collapse a restoration, not a punishment.

---

### Verses 8–12 — Nimrod: the world's first empire-builder

The Nimrod passage is the only narrative interruption in what is otherwise pure genealogical catalogue — and it crackles with a different energy. The Hebrew is dense and rhythmic:

**גִּבֹּר צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה** (*gibbor tsayid lifnei YHWH*) — "a mighty hunter before the LORD."

The phrase *lifnei YHWH* — literally "before the face of the LORD" — is ambiguous in exactly the right way. It can mean "in the sight of the LORD" (i.e., supremely, by divine measure), or "against the face of the LORD" (in opposition to him). The ancient proverb ("Like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD") memorializes him as the archetype of a certain kind of human greatness: raw, physical, empire-founding. The word *gibbor* — mighty warrior/hero — is charged with ambivalence throughout the Hebrew Bible. It is used for the *nephilim* in 6:4, for God's own warriors, for the greatest heroes of Israel. It is power without qualification.

Nimrod's cities establish the geography of human empire: Babel (*Bavel*), Erech (Uruk), Accad — the heartland of Mesopotamian civilization. Then Nineveh — which will become Israel's great nemesis, the city of Jonah's mission, the seat of Assyrian brutality. The text introduces these cities neutrally, even admiringly, as Nimrod's achievements. But for every reader of the Hebrew Bible who encounters this list, the names already carry weight. Babel is where the story fractures in chapter 11. Nineveh is where Jonah runs from. The Table of Nations is planting seeds that will flower across centuries of narrative.

---

### Verse 21 — Shem and the Hebrews

The introduction to Shem's genealogy is the most syntactically complex in the chapter: **לְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם הוּא אֲבִי כָּל בְּנֵי עֵבֶר אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל** — "to Shem also was born — he was the father of all the sons of Eber, the brother of Japheth the elder." The position of "father of all the sons of Eber" before the genealogy even begins signals that this is the sentence's real payload. *Eber* (*עֵבֶר*) is the direct ancestor of the word *Ivri* — "Hebrew." Shem is the ancestor of the ancestor of the Hebrews. The Table of Nations is quietly planting the family line that will eventually reach Abraham (chapter 12). The whole sweep of Japheth's seventy nations is prelude; Shem's line is the spine of the story.

---

### Verse 25 — Peleg and the divided earth

**כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ** — "because in his days the earth was divided." The name Peleg (*פֶּלֶג*) is derived from the root *palag* — to divide, to split, to cleave. This is biblical naming-as-memory: the name Peleg is itself a timestamp. Something happened in his generation that was so significant — so rupturing — that the name of every child born to him became the word for it.

What was the division? The text does not say. Jewish tradition has long associated this with the scattering of Babel (Genesis 11), which occurs in the same narrative sequence and involves the same verb-family. If so, Peleg's very name is a cross-reference pointing forward to the next chapter. The Table of Nations and the Tower of Babel are not two separate stories — they are one story told from two angles: the world divided into nations (chapter 10), and the moment of division explained (chapter 11).

---

### The LXX variant: Cainan

The Septuagint (Codex Vaticanus, page 7, visible in the Gen 10 columns) inserts an extra generation between Arphaxad and Shelah: **Καϊνάν** (*Kainan*). This Cainan appears nowhere in the Masoretic Text (Leningrad Codex) but is consistent across LXX manuscripts and is cited in the New Testament at Luke 3:36, where he appears in the genealogy of Jesus. The LXX translator(s) may have harmonized with an earlier textual tradition, or inserted Cainan to balance the genealogy with the ten-generation structure found elsewhere. The PNEUMA translation follows the Masoretic Text (MT) here — Arphaxad fathered Shelah directly — but readers should know that the Greek tradition witnesses a longer genealogy, and Luke's Gospel follows it.

---

### The structure of the seventy — a theological number

Seventy nations. The number is almost certainly intentional in the final form of the text. Deuteronomy 32:8 refers to the Most High dividing the nations "according to the number of the sons of Israel" — and Israel has seventy members when they go down to Egypt (Exodus 1:5). Seventy elders surround Moses (Exodus 24:1). Seventy is the number of wholeness, of completion, of the full council. The Table of Nations is saying: the whole human family is here, spread out before you, and it is a complete and ordered thing.

From Babel forward, the nations will largely appear in the Hebrew Bible as threats, temptations, and foils to Israel's calling. But the Table of Nations precedes all of that. Before the election of Abraham, before the covenant with Israel, before the land promise — there is this: a God who looks at seventy peoples, each with their clans and languages and territories, and holds them all in one frame. The genealogy is the theology. The nations are not accidents. They are the point.

---

*— PNEUMA*
